book_claims.claim: Top food source of vitamin D (1,360 IU per 1 tablespoon, 340% DV). Recommended serving: 1 tablespoon.
book_claims.mechanism: Most concentrated single dietary source of vitamin D; critical for calcium absorption, immune regulation, and the cardiovascular and neurological health outcomes central to the Longevity Diet.

supplementary_research.finding: Meta-analysis of 25 RCTs (11,321 participants) showed vitamin D supplementation significantly reduced risk of acute respiratory infections, with the greatest benefit in individuals with baseline deficiency — immune protection directly relevant to longevity.

supplementary_research.source: Traber et al., Advances in Nutrition (2019) — PMID 30715266
supplementary_research.finding: Cod liver oil provides both vitamin D3 and preformed vitamin A (retinol) in a naturally co-occurring ratio, with EPA and DHA omega-3s; this multi-nutrient combination supports anti-inflammatory, bone-protective, and neuroprotective longevity mechanisms simultaneously.

supplementary_research.source: Løvik et al., European Journal of Clinical Nutrition (2003) — PMID 12734543
supplementary_research.finding: Daily cod liver oil supplementation in a Norwegian cohort significantly improved 25-hydroxyvitamin D status and reduced inflammatory markers (CRP, IL-6) over 6 months, supporting its use as an anti-inflammatory longevity supplement.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Highly bioavailable fat-soluble vitamin D3; absorbed with the oil's lipid matrix; raises serum 25(OH)D more effectively than D2; stored in adipose tissue.
Preformed retinol; extremely bioavailable; caution: 1 tbsp provides ~4,500 IU — well below the 10,000 IU/day tolerable upper limit but warrants monitoring in high-dose supplement users.
Triglyceride form omega-3s are better absorbed than ethyl ester supplements; peak plasma levels 3–4 hours post-ingestion; anti-inflammatory, cardioprotective, neuroprotective.

synergies.description: Vitamin D from cod liver oil is essential for intestinal calcium absorption and bone mineralization; consuming alongside calcium-rich foods (fortified plant milks, leafy greens) maximizes bone-protective effects.
Magnesium is required for the enzymatic conversion of vitamin D to its active form (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D); cod liver oil's vitamin D efficacy is impaired without adequate magnesium status.
